text.title: LB High Availability Failure Modes
text.summary: 3 antipatterns observed in HA LB designs. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: One AZ goes dark; the ALB stays "healthy" with green target health in the dashboard, but every backend lives in the failed AZ and the service is down.
antipattern.root-cause: Author trusted the LB's multi-AZ subnet list to confer HA without spreading backends across AZs.
antipattern.fix: Spread backends ≥ 2 per AZ across ≥ 2 AZs (or all 3 in us-east-1). Add a deployment policy that fails CI if the target group is healthy in only one AZ.

antipattern.symptom: Region us-east-1 goes down. Route 53 health-check failure triggers a record change with 60 s TTL; mobile customers stay broken for 30+ minutes because their carrier resolver caches DNS for 1 hour.

root-cause.source: AWS Global Accelerator FAQ + DNS TTL behaviour studies

antipattern.root-cause: SLO is 99.99% but the global LB is DNS-based; real-world failover is 30 s – 5 min.
antipattern.fix: Migrate to anycast (AWS Global Accelerator with regional ALB endpoints, or GCP Global LB). Failover happens at BGP layer in < 30 s independent of client DNS caches.

antipattern.symptom: Rolling deploy causes a wave of 502 / connection-reset errors at the load balancer; users see ~10–30 s of intermittent failures per deploy.
antipattern.root-cause: Pod receives SIGTERM and immediately exits; readiness probe never goes 503; the LB keeps sending new requests for one full health-check interval.
antipattern.fix: Add a SIGTERM handler that flips the readiness flag to 503 immediately. Set `terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60`. Set LB `deregistration_delay = 30`. In code, finish in-flight requests before `exit(0)`.
